narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting the likelihood of passing on the first try based on academic and background factors. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, LSAT score of 35, Year 1 school decile of 4, Year 3 decile of 2, upper-middle family income, and full-time student status—and estimated a 47.1% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This elevated risk threshold led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to pass with high confidence. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which is notably lower than the typical student's 5.53 average and signaled weaker performance relative to peers at that stage. In contrast, your undergraduate GPA, below the average of 3.21, had a moderating effect but still pulled somewhat away from a strong pass prediction. Your LSAT score of 35, while solid, also fell short of the dataset average of 36.70 and contributed modestly against failure, helping to keep your risk from being even higher. Compared to typical students, your earlier Year 1 decile of 4 was closer to average (5.71), but the sharp drop by Year 3 stood out as the dominant concern, outweighing positives like your full-time commitment and family background. This pattern suggests the model saw your later academic trajectory as the biggest red flag for bar exam success. While your Asian background and male gender are part of the broader dataset context, the decision hinged on these performance metrics rather than demographics. To move forward, focus on strengthening your academic record—perhaps by retaking courses or pursuing targeted LSAT prep to boost these key areas—and consider reapplying after demonstrating improvement, as many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing such gaps. Schools value resilience, so gathering letters of explanation or additional certifications could help contextualize your profile next time. With deliberate steps, you can shift this prediction in your favor.
